edibility: Highly edible and prized as a choice mushroom. Young, firm specimens have pleasant, slightly crunchy texture and mild flavor. However, the edibility depends critically on the host: Hypomyces lactifluorum parasitizes Russula brevipes and Lactarius piperatus (both safe), but theoretically could infect toxic Russula species—always verify the host identity before consuming. No confirmed toxin cases despite this risk.
taste: Mild, slightly sweet with a crunchy texture when young. Develops nutty, earthy notes with age. Firm flesh becomes soft and undesirable when overripe.
search: Found in conifer and hardwood forests (oak, maple, beech, ponderosa pine) from late July to October, extending into November in the Pacific Northwest. Identify by bright orange-red hard exterior with tiny pimples covering the host mushroom; internally white with blunt, chanterelle-like ridges. Locate by finding white Russula or Lactarius species beneath the orange parasite coating.

toxicity.symptoms: N/A — not toxic. Rare GI distress reported in sensitive individuals, but not poisoning. Risk exists only if host is toxic Russula species (uncommon in North America).
